perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be inadequate, and hence, RECIPIENT agrees that HALO shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including injunction, in the event of breach by RECIPIENT.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be inadequate.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that HALO is entitled to equitable remedies, including injunction, limits the legal recourse available to HALO, potentially contradicting California law regarding remedies for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3366 states that specific performance or an injunction may be granted to enforce any obligation. By removing the clause explicitly granting HALO the right to seek an injunction, the modified text may be interpreted as a waiver of this right, which could be challenged under California law, especially if monetary damages are insufficient.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3366 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. General Principles [3366 - 3369] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 3366. Specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state. (Amended by Stats. 1905, Ch. 465.)
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states HALO is entitled to equitable remedies, including injunction, for breach by the RECIPIENT. The changed text removes this entitlement. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code § 3366 allows for injunctions to enforce obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating that "Specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state." By removing HALO's explicit right to seek an injunction, the modified contract potentially contradicts California law, which allows for such remedies, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined by an Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (excluding the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of Califor nia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Califor nia.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase '(excluding the laws of conflict of laws)' potentially creates ambiguity regarding which state's laws will govern if there is a dispute involving parties from multiple states. This omission could contradict California's choice-of-law rules.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1646, in conjunction with case law, dictates how choice-of-law provisions are interpreted. By omitting the exclusion of conflict of laws principles, the agreement may inadvertently trigger a conflict of laws analysis, potentially leading to a different state's laws being applied if California's conflict of laws rules dictate that another state has a more significant relationship to the agreement or the parties. This introduces ambiguity and potential contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly excludes 'the laws of conflict of laws,' which provides clarity. The changed text removes this exclusion, potentially triggering a conflict of laws analysis, as the Law Explanation suggests. This omission, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation, introduces ambiguity and the potential application of a different state's laws, which contradicts the initial intent to be governed solely by California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Civil Code § 1646, states that a contract is interpreted according to the law of the place of performance or where it is made, but it doesn't address the exclusion of conflict of laws, thus reinforcing the importance of the omitted phrase in the original text to avoid ambiguity in choice of law.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees that RECIPIENT will use reasonable care to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific 5-year confidentiality period and the limitation on dissemination only to employees involved in the evaluation weakens the protection of HALO's confidential information. This could contradict California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) if the confidential information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets. Removing the 5-year confidentiality period and employee restriction makes it harder for HALO to prove misappropriation if RECIPIENT discloses the information after a shorter period or through employees not involved in the evaluation, which undermines HALO's ability to enforce its trade secret rights under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the 5-year confidentiality period and the restriction on dissemination to only employees involved in the evaluation. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this weakens the protection of HALO's confidential information and undermines HALO's ability to enforce its trade secret rights under California law. This falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), giving HALO the right to protect their trade secrets. By removing these restrictions, RECIPIENT can now disseminate the information after a shorter period, or to non-involved employees, harming HALO. Removing the 5 year limitation and employee restriction, directly contradicts the original intent, as it removes protections that were originally present.

perturbation.original_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, remain the property of HALO; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to HALO upon its written or destroyed by RECIPIENT at HALO’s option.
perturbation.changed_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall remain the property of HALO.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause about returning or destroying the information, the contract becomes ambiguous about the RECIPIENT's obligations regarding the physical copies of confidential information after the evaluation period. This could conflict with California law regarding the duty to protect trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.2 addresses injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. While this section doesn't explicitly mandate the return or destruction of information, failing to specify these actions weakens HALO's ability to obtain an injunction to prevent ongoing misuse of the information, as it leaves open the possibility of RECIPIENT retaining copies. This ambiguity increases HALO's legal risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the requirement for the recipient to return or destroy confidential information, which creates a legal loophole. The Law Explanation suggests this weakens HALO's ability to obtain an injunction to prevent misuse of the information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Civil Code § 3426.2, subsection (c), states that 'affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order.' By removing the obligation to return or destroy the information, the contract fails to provide a clear mechanism for HALO to ensure the protection of its trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the court's ability to compel affirmative acts for protection.
